Title: Run a Pre-Mortem on a Plan Before You Commit
Teaser: Imagines your plan has already failed spectacularly, then works backward to surface the failure modes you're currently blind to and how to defuse them now.
PromptHint: Describe the decision or plan in [PLAN]. Add the timeframe and what 'success' looks like in [CONTEXT] if you can.
Prompt:
You are a seasoned operator who has watched many confident plans fail, and you run pre-mortems to catch problems while they're still cheap to fix. Here is the plan I'm about to commit to:
<plan>
[PLAN]
</plan>
Context, timeframe, and what success would look like (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: assume it is one year from now and this plan has clearly failed. Don't soften it — picture the specific, embarrassing version of failure.
Then write the pre-mortem: (1) A vivid one-paragraph 'failure story' narrating how it went wrong. (2) A ranked list of the distinct failure modes behind that story, each tagged by likelihood (High/Med/Low) and how visible the warning signs would be (Obvious / Subtle / Silent). (3) For each High or Silent risk, the single earliest leading indicator I could watch for. (4) Concrete pre-commitments or design changes I can make NOW to remove or shrink the top 3 risks. (5) One 'kill criterion' — the specific condition under which I should stop or reverse.
CONSTRAINTS: Attack the plan, not me. No generic risks like 'poor execution' — name the specific mechanism. Don't invent facts about my situation; if a risk depends on an unknown, say so.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Failure story' paragraph, a Risks table (Failure mode | Likelihood | Warning visibility | Earliest indicator | Mitigation now), then a 'Kill criterion' line.

Title: Build a Weighted Trade-Off Matrix for Your Options
Teaser: Turns a fuzzy 'which one should I pick' into a transparent scored matrix where the weights, scores, and the winner are all defensible.
PromptHint: List the options to compare in [OPTIONS]. Name what matters most to you in [CRITERIA] (or let the model propose criteria).
Prompt:
You are a decision analyst who makes choices auditable instead of relying on a gut feeling people can't question. Options on the table:
<options>
[OPTIONS]
</options>
What matters to me / criteria (optional — propose sensible ones if blank): [CRITERIA]
My situation and any hard constraints: [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: choose 4-7 decision criteria, assign each a weight that sums to 100, and decide a clear 0-10 scoring rubric so scores aren't arbitrary.
Then build the matrix: (1) State the weighted criteria and one line on why each weight. (2) Score every option 0-10 on each criterion, with a half-line justification per score, marking any score that's a guess. (3) Compute the weighted total for each option and rank them. (4) Run a sensitivity check: name the one weight that, if I changed it, would flip the ranking — so I know how fragile the result is. (5) Give a plain-language recommendation, plus the strongest case for the runner-up.
CONSTRAINTS: Weights must sum to 100; show the math. Don't fabricate facts about the options — if a score needs info I didn't give, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT'. The numbers serve the decision; if the matrix says one thing but it feels wrong, flag the gap rather than hiding it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A weights list, a scoring matrix table (Criterion | Weight | each option's score), a ranked totals line, a 'Sensitivity' note, and a 'Recommendation' paragraph.

Title: Red-Team My Plan Like an Adversary Who Wants It to Fail
Teaser: Hands your plan to a sharp, hostile critic whose only job is to find the holes, the weakest assumption, and the attack that breaks it.
PromptHint: Paste the plan, strategy, or argument in [PLAN]. Add who could oppose it or what could go wrong in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a red-team strategist hired to break my plan before reality does. You are skeptical, specific, and unsentimental — but fair. The plan:
<plan>
[PLAN]
</plan>
Who might oppose it, and the environment it operates in (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: identify the load-bearing assumption — the one belief that, if false, makes the whole plan collapse. Then think like a competitor, a skeptical boss, and an unlucky calendar.
Then attack: (1) Name the single weakest assumption and explain exactly how it could be wrong. (2) List the 5 strongest objections, steel-manned (state each as its smartest version, not a strawman). (3) Describe the 'easiest way this gets killed' — the most likely real-world path to failure, not the scariest. (4) Identify who loses if this succeeds and how they'd push back. (5) For each major weakness, either a fix or an honest 'no good fix — accept this risk'.
CONSTRAINTS: No flattery and no 'looks great overall'. Don't manufacture objections that don't apply — quality over quantity. If the plan is actually solid on a point, say so briefly and move on.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Weakest assumption' paragraph, a numbered 'Top objections (steel-manned)' list each with a counter or 'accept the risk', an 'Easiest way this dies' paragraph, and a final 'Verdict: proceed / proceed with changes / rethink' line with one sentence of why.

Title: Map the Second- and Third-Order Consequences
Teaser: Pushes a decision past the obvious first result into the ripple effects, the 'and then what' chains, and the consequences that bite later.
PromptHint: Describe the decision or action in [DECISION]. Add who's affected and the time horizon in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a systems thinker who is wary of decisions that look good immediately and bad eventually. The decision under consideration:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Who is affected, the environment, and the time horizon (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: for each likely first effect, keep asking 'and then what happens?' two more levels deep, including how other people will react and adapt.
Then map the consequences: (1) List the obvious first-order effects (what happens immediately). (2) For each, trace the second-order effects — including how others respond and what incentives shift. (3) Push the most important chains to third-order effects, where the non-obvious surprises usually live. (4) Flag any effect that is positive now but negative later (or vice versa), and any feedback loop that could compound. (5) Name the one second- or third-order effect I'm most likely to be ignoring, and what to do about it.
CONSTRAINTS: Be concrete about mechanisms ('X causes Y because…'), not vague ('this could have ripple effects'). Distinguish likely from speculative. Don't assume the worst by default — include plausible upside chains too.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A consequence tree as nested bullets (1st → 2nd → 3rd order), a short 'Time-delayed reversals & feedback loops' list, and a final 'The effect you're underweighting' callout with a recommended response.

Title: Stress-Test a Decision Against Three Future Scenarios
Teaser: Pressure-checks your choice across an optimistic, expected, and adverse future so you pick something that holds up no matter which one arrives.
PromptHint: Describe the decision and the leading option in [DECISION]. Add the key uncertainties you're worried about in [UNKNOWNS].
Prompt:
You are a strategist who plans for the world that shows up, not the world you hoped for. The decision and my current leaning:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
The big uncertainties or things outside my control (optional): [UNKNOWNS]
Think first, silently: identify the 2-3 uncertainties that most drive the outcome, then build three coherent futures around them — not just good/bad, but internally consistent stories.
Then stress-test: (1) Define three scenarios — Tailwind (things break my way), Base case (boringly likely), and Headwind (the realistic rough version) — each with the specific conditions that define it. (2) For each scenario, judge how my current choice performs and what would hurt most. (3) Identify which option is 'robust' (acceptable across all three) versus 'fragile' (great in one, disastrous in another). (4) Surface no-regret moves I can make now that pay off in every scenario. (5) Name the early signal that would tell me which scenario is actually unfolding, so I can adapt in time.
CONSTRAINTS: Headwind is the realistic bad case, not the apocalypse — keep it plausible. Don't assign fake probabilities with false precision; rough bands ('more likely than not') are fine. Tie advice back to the specific scenarios.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three scenario blocks (Conditions | How my choice fares | What hurts most), a 'Robust vs fragile' verdict, a 'No-regret moves' list, and a 'Signals to watch' list.

Title: Surface and Test the Assumptions Holding Up My Decision
Teaser: Excavates the hidden beliefs your decision quietly depends on, ranks them by how dangerous they are if wrong, and tells you which to test first.
PromptHint: Describe the decision and your reasoning in [DECISION]. Add any facts you're treating as given in [GIVENS].
Prompt:
You are a critical-thinking partner who knows most bad decisions trace back to one unexamined assumption. My decision and the reasoning behind it:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Things I'm currently treating as true / given (optional): [GIVENS]
Think first, silently: separate what I actually know from what I'm assuming, including the assumptions so obvious I didn't state them.
Then audit the assumptions: (1) List every assumption the decision rests on, including the implicit ones I didn't mention. (2) For each, classify it as Fact (verified), Belief (probably true), or Hope (convenient but untested). (3) Score each on two axes: how central it is to the decision, and how confident I should honestly be in it. (4) Flag the 'load-bearing risky' assumptions — central AND shaky — because those are where to spend effort. (5) For the top 2-3, propose the cheapest, fastest test or evidence I could gather to confirm or kill it before committing.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't accept my framing uncritically — challenge a premise if it deserves it. Be honest when something I called a fact is actually a belief. No vague tests like 'do more research' — name the specific check.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An assumptions table (Assumption | Fact/Belief/Hope | Centrality | Confidence), a 'Load-bearing risky' shortlist, and a 'Test these first' list with the specific cheap test for each.

Title: Decide Faster With a Reversibility & Stakes Filter
Teaser: Sorts a decision by how reversible it is and how much is at stake, so you stop agonizing over cheap two-way doors and slow down for the real one-way ones.
PromptHint: Describe the decision you're stuck on in [DECISION]. Add the deadline and what you're afraid of getting wrong in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a pragmatic advisor who helps people match their deliberation to what a decision actually deserves. The decision I'm wrestling with:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Deadline, and what I'm most afraid of getting wrong (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: assess two things — how reversible this is (a one-way door you can't easily undo, or a two-way door you can walk back), and how high the real stakes are if it goes wrong.
Then guide the decision: (1) Classify it on the reversibility-by-stakes grid and say which quadrant it's in. (2) Give a clear verdict on how much process this deserves: decide fast and move on, set a short time-box, or slow down and do real diligence — with the reasoning. (3) If it's a reversible low-stakes decision, push me to just choose and name a good-enough default so I stop overthinking. (4) If it's irreversible and high-stakes, list the 3 things I genuinely must get right and a simple checkpoint before I commit. (5) Identify any way to make an irreversible decision more reversible (a pilot, a trial, a smaller first step, an exit clause).
CONSTRAINTS: Don't add ceremony to a small decision or rush a big one. Be decisive — give an actual recommendation, not 'it depends'. No invented details about my situation.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Quadrant' line, a 'How much process this deserves' verdict with reasoning, the relevant action list for that quadrant, and a 'Ways to make it more reversible' note.

Title: Pick the Right Decision Framework for This Problem
Teaser: Diagnoses what kind of decision you're actually facing and prescribes the framework that fits it, instead of forcing every choice through the same template.
PromptHint: Describe the decision and why it feels hard in [DECISION]. Add what you've already tried or considered in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a decision coach who knows the wrong framework wastes time and the right one cuts through. The decision and why it feels hard:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
What I've already tried or am considering (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: diagnose the real shape of this decision — is it many options on clear criteria, a binary go/no-go, a bet under deep uncertainty, a values conflict, or a reversible experiment? The shape dictates the tool.
Then prescribe: (1) Name what type of decision this actually is, in one or two sentences. (2) Recommend the single best-fit framework (e.g., weighted matrix, expected-value reasoning, pre-mortem, 10/10/10, two-way-door test, OODA, cost-of-delay) and explain in plain language why it fits THIS problem. (3) Walk me through applying it to my specific case, step by step, doing as much of the work as my input allows. (4) Name one common framework that would be a poor fit here and why, so I don't reach for it. (5) End with the concrete next action the framework points to.
CONSTRAINTS: Recommend one primary framework, not a buffet — at most mention a secondary. Explain frameworks in everyday terms; assume no jargon. Don't pad a simple decision with heavy process.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'What kind of decision this is' paragraph, 'Recommended framework + why it fits', a numbered 'Applied to your case' walkthrough, a 'Don't use ___ here' note, and a 'Next action' line.

Title: Run a Devil's Advocate Debate on Both Sides
Teaser: Stages a sharp internal debate where one voice argues hard for your decision and another argues hard against, then a neutral judge calls it.
PromptHint: State the decision as a yes/no or this-vs-that in [DECISION]. Add the context and stakes in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are facilitating a rigorous internal debate to protect me from my own confirmation bias. The decision, framed as a clear choice:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Context, constraints, and stakes (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: build the strongest honest case for each side, drawing on what actually matters here, not generic pros and cons.
Then run the debate in three voices: (1) ADVOCATE — argues forcefully FOR the decision: the best reasons, the upside, why hesitation is the real risk. (2) SKEPTIC — argues forcefully AGAINST: the hidden costs, the failure modes, why this is a trap. Each side gets its best shot, steel-manned, no strawmen. (3) Let each respond once to the other's single strongest point. (4) JUDGE — a neutral synthesis: which case is actually stronger and why, what evidence would change the verdict, and the conditions under which each side wins. (5) A final recommendation with a confidence level (low/medium/high) and the one thing I should check before acting.
CONSTRAINTS: Both sides must be genuinely strong — don't secretly stack the deck toward the 'obvious' answer. The judge must take a position, not sit on the fence. No invented facts; reason from what I gave you.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'ADVOCATE' section, 'SKEPTIC' section, a brief 'Rebuttals' exchange, a 'JUDGE' synthesis, and a 'Recommendation + confidence' line.

Title: Expose the Hidden and Opportunity Costs of a Choice
Teaser: Drags the invisible costs of a decision into the light — what you give up, what it crowds out, and the price tags nobody put on the spreadsheet.
PromptHint: Describe the option you're leaning toward in [OPTION]. Add what else you could do with the same time/money/energy in [ALTERNATIVES].
Prompt:
You are a clear-eyed advisor who knows the real cost of a choice is everything it quietly forecloses. The option I'm leaning toward:
<option>
[OPTION]
</option>
What else I could do with the same time, money, or energy (optional): [ALTERNATIVES]
My situation and resources: [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: look past the sticker price to the costs that don't show up on an invoice — time, attention, optionality, relationships, energy, and the best thing I'd be saying no to.
Then expose the full cost: (1) The obvious direct costs (money, time) as I'd normally count them. (2) The hidden costs — switching costs, maintenance, stress, learning curve, reputation, the 'tax' of complexity. (3) The opportunity cost: the single best alternative use of these resources, and what choosing this means I give up. (4) Optionality effects — does this open future doors or quietly close them? (5) A blunt bottom line: once ALL costs are counted, does the upside still clearly justify it, and what would have to be true for it to be worth it?
CONSTRAINTS: Don't inflate costs to scare me or ignore real benefits — be balanced. Quantify where I gave numbers; otherwise describe magnitude (small/moderate/large) without inventing figures. Name the specific alternative being given up, not 'other things'.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A costs table (Cost type | What it is | Rough magnitude), an 'Opportunity cost' paragraph, an 'Effect on future options' note, and a 'Bottom line: worth it if…' conclusion.

Title: Cut Through Analysis Paralysis to a Committed Decision
Teaser: For when you've researched enough and are still stuck — forces a clean, committed call with a default, a deadline, and a way to stop second-guessing.
PromptHint: Describe the decision and what's keeping you stuck in [DECISION]. List the options you're torn between in [OPTIONS].
Prompt:
You are a decisive mentor who helps people who've already gathered enough information finally choose and move. The decision I'm frozen on:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
The options I'm torn between (optional): [OPTIONS]
What keeps me stuck (fear, perfectionism, missing info, others' opinions): [STUCK]
Think first, silently: figure out whether I'm genuinely missing decision-relevant information, or whether I have enough and am stalling for emotional reasons. Treat those very differently.
Then unstick me: (1) Diagnose honestly: is more information actually needed, or am I procrastinating on a good-enough call? (2) If information is genuinely missing, name the ONE piece that would move the needle and the fastest way to get it — and a deadline to stop looking. (3) If I have enough, say so plainly and identify what's really blocking me (fear of regret, others' judgment, perfectionism). (4) Give a recommended choice with a one-paragraph rationale — take a stand. (5) Hand me a 'commitment kit': the decision stated in one line, a date to commit by, a pre-decided response if it goes wrong, and permission to stop revisiting it.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't enable more endless research when the real issue is fear — call it. Be willing to recommend an actual option, not 'whichever feels right'. This is decision support, not professional advice; for major financial, medical, or legal choices, consult a qualified expert before committing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Diagnosis: more info needed vs. just stalling', the relevant unblocking step, a 'Recommended choice + why', and a 'Commitment kit' (one-line decision, commit-by date, if-it-goes-wrong plan, stop-revisiting rule).

Title: Make a High-Stakes Decision Anyone Else Could Audit
Teaser: Produces a full, written decision record — the question, the options, the reasoning, the risks, and the call — so the choice is transparent and defensible later.
PromptHint: State the decision to be made in [DECISION]. Add the realistic options and the key constraints or stakeholders in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a decision facilitator producing a written record that a thoughtful outsider — or my future self — could read and understand exactly why this call was made. The decision:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Realistic options, constraints, stakeholders, and deadline (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: nail down the actual question being decided (people often answer a different one than they asked), then lay out the options and reasoning so the logic is visible, not buried.
Then write the decision record: (1) DECISION QUESTION — the precise call to be made, in one sentence. (2) CONTEXT — the situation, constraints, and what's driving the timing. (3) OPTIONS CONSIDERED — each realistic option with its core trade-off, including the 'do nothing' option. (4) KEY FACTORS — the 3-5 things that actually decide this, weighted by importance. (5) RECOMMENDATION — the chosen option with explicit reasoning tying back to the key factors. (6) RISKS & MITIGATIONS — what could go wrong and the plan for each. (7) HOW WE'LL KNOW — the signal that tells us this was right or wrong, and when to review.
CONSTRAINTS: Make the reasoning explicit — no 'because it's obviously best'. Always include the status-quo / do-nothing option. Mark anything that's an assumption rather than a known fact. Don't invent stakeholders or numbers I didn't provide.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections above as Markdown headings, options as a short table (Option | Core trade-off), and a final one-line 'Decision' statement plus a 'Review date / trigger'.
